But it wasn't just outcome data, it was also - proximity to home was very important because the first year, especially after a heart transplant, you need to constantly come in and Columbia was closer to our home.
And my son had been treated there all his life, so he felt comfortable there.

Talking with Doron Weber, author of ""Immortal Bird: A Family Memoir,"" on SCIENCE FRIDAY from NPR.
You write in the book that you were a very private - you were very private about your son's illness, that you hadn't even shared it with your assistants at work, people who you know.
Why then did you decide to make it public and go public with it?
Well, once Damon was dead, it was - I felt that it was the only way to - as I said, to bring him back and to share his life, and there was no other way to tell the story.
And I think while it was going on, it was so overwhelming, but the only way to handle it was to, you know, maintain an external life and try to cordon it off.
Mm-hmm.
There are so many moments in this book where you're responding to a crisis.
Your son's blood pressure is dropping rapidly.
At one point his heart stops.
He's getting CPR.
You describe these events in amazing detail.
How do you remember?
How did you remember all of these things?
What's amazing is that the mind is actually - records everything.
And if you just spend enough time and you focus, what you discover is nothing is ever lost.
And so this was a process.
And one of the amazing things I learned in writing this book was that nothing goes away, and you just sort of peel back layers and layers and you just sit there.
And by the, you know, after a couple of hours, you feel like you're literally living it or reliving it and you're in the moment, and it seems like it's a verbatim conversation that you're hearing.
Mm-hmm.
You have other children?
Yes, Sam and Miranda.

It's probably too early to tell, but my son Sam and I have discussed it.
And he, you know, he said - to me, anyway - many positive things about it.

And I think there are obviously things in it that he couldn't have known at the time, though when he said that to me he said, well, Dad, I knew - I kind of knew most of it, so I don't - I can't tell how much of it was, you know, was new to him or not.
And I think it's going to take years, really, for the full impact to register.
So it's a question I could answer in 10 years better, perhaps.
Mm-hmm.

The names come from the so-called Barbados Slave Register of 1834.

NPR's Tony Cox spoke with Simon Ziviani of ancestry.com, and Zibiani explained what makes the Barbados registry so special.
This database is very reliable.
Barbados in 1834 was very much under the rule of the British; therefore, all the plantations did submit registers at that time.

Other colonial dependencies, they didn't have as tight control, and of course the plantation owners weren't particularly willing in many instances to fill in these registers because they wanted to continue owning and trading in slaves.
So some registers in some dependencies aren't as complete.
What happened to those British slave owners who did not comply with the law?
Well, there were penalties.
They could have the slaves taken away from them.
There could be financial penalties.
But again, in many instances, none of this was applied to those plantation owners because these are - we're talking about very remote parts of the world, and to enforce the law was very difficult.
To people who go this register, what information will they find?
What is there available for them?
The information available in this register is the name of the plantation owner and the slaves, their list of slaves.

So you'll find the name of the plantation, the owner, their family, the list of Christian names of the slaves, their ages, their places of birth and what they did on their particular plantation - 100,000 names, 99,000 of which are the slaves in Barbados.
